questions.a: A unique numeric identifier for a running process.
It creates a child process by duplicating the caller.

questions.q: What does exec() do after fork()?
questions.a: It replaces the current process image with a new program.
A process that has exited but still has an entry until the parent reaps it.
A running child whose parent has exited, so init or a subreaper adopts it.
Saving one process state and restoring another so the CPU can switch between them.

questions.q: Why are fork() and exec() separate calls?
questions.a: Fork creates a configurable child, and exec runs a new program after setup is complete.

questions.q: Why must a parent call wait() or waitpid()?
questions.a: To collect the child's exit status and release its zombie process entry.

questions.q: What happens to open file descriptors across fork()?
questions.a: The child inherits copies of the parent's descriptors pointing to the same open file descriptions.

questions.q: Why can a shell not ignore SIGCHLD forever?
questions.a: It would leak zombie children because exited processes would never be reaped.

questions.q: What is the purpose of a process group?
questions.a: It lets the terminal or shell send signals to a related set of processes together.

questions.q: Why is a blocking system call significant to scheduling?
questions.a: The kernel can deschedule the process and run something else while it waits.

questions.q: Why do shells use fork/exec instead of spawning a brand new interpreter each time?
questions.a: It is cheaper and preserves shell state such as environment, redirections, and job control.

questions.q: When would you prefer processes over threads?
questions.a: When you want stronger isolation, separate failure domains, or simpler memory safety boundaries.

questions.q: What is the tradeoff of forking a large process tree?
questions.a: It is flexible, but many children increase scheduler overhead and memory pressure.

questions.q: Why does a daemon often double-fork?
questions.a: To detach from the controlling terminal and avoid becoming a zombie-prone session child.

questions.q: What tradeoff does graceful shutdown introduce?
questions.a: It improves correctness and cleanup, but it can delay termination and complicate signal handling.

questions.q: Why can a process pool be better than one process per task?
questions.a: It amortizes startup cost and limits resource usage while still providing parallelism.
